coredata.dc:title: Amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH2-SQDs) for detection of tetracycline in food samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Jothi Vinoth
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH₂-SQDs) are introduced as a novel and highly efficient fluorescent probe for detecting tetracycline (TC) in food and environmental samples. Unlike traditional carbon quantum dots, NH₂-SQDs exhibit excellent photoluminescence stability, a high quantum yield of 59.5 %, and enhanced selectivity. Synthesized through an ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal method, these NH₂-SQDs attain an ultra-low detection limit of 0.003 μmol/L by utilizing fluorescence quenching mechanisms, including hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and the inner filter effect. This method surpasses conventional fluorescence-based tetracycline sensors by providing greater sensitivity, strong anti-interference capability, and a quick response time. Validation with real samples, such as milk, honey, and water, showed high recovery rates ranging from 94.7 % to 100.5 % with minimal standard deviation, reinforcing the reliability of the results. These findings suggest that NH₂-SQDs can serve as a cost-effective and robust alternative for real-time monitoring of antibiotic residues, thereby improving food safety and environmental outcomes protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amine-functionalized
Sulfur quantum dots
Tetracycline detection
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: NH₂-SQD as a fluorescent probe for tetracycline detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) ( 2022R1A2B5B02001422 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NH2-SQDs, (b) excitation and emission spectra of NH2-SQDs, and (c) emission behavior at varying excitation wavelengths.
(a) HR-TEM image, (b) lattice fringe, and (c) histogram of NH2-SQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of NH2-SQDs, (b) elemental percentage, (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) S2p, (f) O1s.

(a) Fluorescence response of NH₂-SQDs in the presence of various antibiotics. (b) Sensitivity analysis depicting fluorescence quenching in response to increasing tetracycline concentrations, ranging from 0 to 100 µM. (c) Non-linear Stern-Volmer plot demonstrating the quenching behavior of N-doped carbon dots by tetracycline. (d) Linear Stern-Volmer plot for tetracycline concentrations between 0 and 60 µM. (e) Photographic images of selectivity for tetracycline.
Lifetime of NH₂-SQDs with TC and without TC.
(a) Quencher efficiency, (b) Interference evaluation of NH₂-SQDs in the presence of tetracycline, (c) Ionic strength of tetracycline, (d) effect of various solvents.
Comparison of NH₂-SQD with other tetracycline detection methods.
Detection of tetracycline from environmental samples.

head.ce:title: Amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH2-SQDs) for detection of tetracycline in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Applied Nanomaterials, Chennai Institute of Technology, Chennai, 600 069, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Applied Nanomaterials, Chennai Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCentre for Applied Nanomaterials, Chennai Institute of Technology, Chennai, 600 069, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bBioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH₂-SQDs) are introduced as a novel and highly efficient fluorescent probe for detecting tetracycline (TC) in food and environmental samples. Unlike traditional carbon quantum dots, NH₂-SQDs exhibit excellent photoluminescence stability, a high quantum yield of 59.5 %, and enhanced selectivity. Synthesized through an ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal method, these NH₂-SQDs attain an ultra-low detection limit of 0.003 μmol/L by utilizing fluorescence quenching mechanisms, including hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and the inner filter effect. This method surpasses conventional fluorescence-based tetracycline sensors by providing greater sensitivity, strong anti-interference capability, and a quick response time. Validation with real samples, such as milk, honey, and water, showed high recovery rates ranging from 94.7 % to 100.5 % with minimal standard deviation, reinforcing the reliability of the results. These findings suggest that NH₂-SQDs can serve as a cost-effective and robust alternative for real-time monitoring of antibiotic residues, thereby improving food safety and environmental outcomes protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic residues in food products are a major public health concern due to their potential impacts on human health [1]. These impacts include allergic reactions, disruption of gut microbiota, and, most critically, the development of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. The consumption of foods contaminated with antibiotics can lead to the ingestion of sub-therapeutic levels of these drugs, which may promote the growth of antibiotic-resistant microorganisms [2]. This issue is particularly concerning as it can undermine the effectiveness of antibiotics, which are crucial in treating bacterial infections. Tetracycline is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that is widely used in veterinary medicine to treat and prevent infections in livestock. Its extensive use can lead to its presence in various food products, such as milk, meat, and eggs. Regulatory bodies have established maximum residue limits (MRLs) for antibiotics in food to ensure consumer safety. However, the monitoring and enforcement of these limits require effective and reliable detection methods [3,4]. The Traditional Detection Methods, Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS), High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC), Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA), microbiological assays, sensitive, specific, rapid, cost-effective, and user-friendly [5].

Tetracycline, a broad-spectrum antibiotic, is frequently used in animal medicine, forestry, therapeutic therapy, and animal husbandry. Nevertheless, the overuse of tetracycline exacerbates drug resistance development and presents a serious threat to the environment following wastewater treatment discharge [6]. These days, HPLC, electrophoresis by capillary, and electrochemistry are frequently used tetracycline detection techniques. The fluorescent sensor is unquestionably the most promising detection technique due to its ease of use and quick detection speed, even when taking into account the experimental expense, environmental contamination from a large volume of organic mobile phase of HPLC, and the low reproducibility of electrochemistry [7]. Numerous fluorescence sensors have been described for tetracycline detection, with the majority relying on carbon quantum dots doped with N and S atoms. Although the excitation-dependent fluorescence of these carbon dots may be significant for some real-world uses, their broad emission spectra and generally poor color purity severely restrict their practical use [8].

Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) represent a new class of metal-free quantum dots that offer several advantages over traditional quantum dots, including good water solubility, low toxicity, and outstanding biocompatibility [9]. Unlike carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which often suffer from broad emission spectra and excitation-dependent fluorescence, SQDs provide more stable photoluminescence properties. Their straightforward and environmentally friendly synthesis has made them an emerging class of green fluorescent nanomaterials, attracting significant interest in recent years. The first report on SQDs was published by Li's group in 2014, in which they synthesized SQDs by gradually oxidizing sulfide ions (S²⁻) from CdS QDs using nitric acid (HNO₃) as an oxidant [10]. However, this method had significant limitations, including the production of only blue-light emission and a remarkably low quantum yield (QY) of 0.549 %, restricting its practical applications in sensing and bioimaging. Later, in 2018, Shen's team introduced a top-down approach based on an "assembly-fission" reaction to transform sublimated sulfur into SQDs. While this method achieved a slightly improved QY of 3.8 %, it required an exceptionally long synthesis time of 125 h-making it impractical for large-scale applications. Other research efforts have explored modifications to SQD synthesis to enhance their optical and structural properties.

Various precursors, such as sodium sulfide (Na₂S), thiourea, and elemental sulfur, have been utilized to improve QY, adjust emission wavelengths, and enhance functionalization [11]. However, many of these approaches still face challenges, such as poor fluorescence stability, susceptibility to photobleaching, and complex purification steps. Additionally, most reported SQD-based probes lack excitation-independent fluorescence, which is a crucial factor for practical sensing applications in real-world matrices like food and environmental samples. Despite the promising potential of SQDs for fluorescence-based sensing, their applications in detecting antibiotics, particularly tetracycline, remain underexplored [12]. Conventional quantum dot-based sensors, which primarily use nitrogen- and sulfur-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs), have demonstrated capabilities for tetracycline detection. However, they often face challenges such as limited reproducibility and signal stability in complex sample environments due to excitation-dependent fluorescence.

Additionally, these sensors show broad emission spectra, reducing both color purity and the signal-to-noise ratio, which ultimately affects detection accuracy. Furthermore, low quantum yields restrict sensitivity, necessitating higher probe concentrations for reliable detection. Another significant drawback is the lack of selectivity, as many carbon-based quantum dots exhibit fluorescence quenching for various antibiotic residues, resulting in potential false positives.

This study introduces amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH₂-SQDs) synthesized via a hydrothermal ethylenediamine-assisted method. This approach not only enhances photoluminescence stability and quantum yield (59.5 %) but also ensures strong selectivity for tetracycline through fluorescence quenching mechanisms involving hydrogen bonding and the inner filter effect (IFE). The amine functionalization further improves interaction specificity with tetracycline, overcoming the non-selective nature of previous quantum dot-based probes. Moreover, the simple and eco-friendly synthesis process makes NH₂-SQDs a cost-effective and scalable alternative for antibiotic residue detection in food safety and environmental monitoring. By integrating these advancements, this study connects the theoretical possibilities with the practical applications of SQDs in fluorescence sensing. It demonstrates a platform that offers high efficiency, selectivity, and sensitivity for the real-time detection of tetracycline in complex food and environmental matrices.

Kanamycin (C18H36N4O11·H2O4S), Amoxicillin (C16H19N3O5S·3H2O), Ampicillin (C16H19N3O4S), Chlortetracycline (C22H23ClN2O8), Tetracycline (C22H24N2O8·xH2O) and Gentamicin (C21H43N5O7) and other common ions (Zinc Nitrate (Zn(NO₃)₂), Manganese(II) nitrate (Mn(NO₃)₂), Magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), Potassium nitrate (KNO3), Iron(III) nitrate (Fe(NO₃)₃), Cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2), and Calcium nitrate (Ca(NO₃)₂). Ethylene diamine and sublimated sulfur from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory in Tianjin, China. Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China) supplied the hydrogen peroxide, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, and disodium hydrogen phosphate. From Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China), sodium hydroxide was acquired. All of the reagents employed, unless otherwise noted, were analytically pure. Ultrapure water from a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA, FRA) was used to prepare all solutions.

The NH₂-SQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal, ethylenediamine-assisted approach to achieve high fluorescence quantum yield and stability. Specifically, 1 g of sulfur powder was dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol with continuous stirring at room temperature to ensure complete dissolution. Ethanol was chosen as a solvent due to its capacity to dissolve sulfur while minimizing oxidation and promoting uniform nucleation. To introduce amine functionalization, 5 mL of ethylenediamine (EDA) was added dropwise to the sulfur solution while stirring, facilitating the incorporation of nitrogen into the quantum dots, thereby enhancing their solubility and interaction with tetracycline. The reaction mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 80 °C for 10 h. The temperature was optimized to ensure controlled nucleation and growth of SQDs while preventing excessive particle aggregation. Higher temperatures (>100 °C) could lead to uncontrolled growth and size variation, while lower temperatures (<60 °C) might not provide sufficient energy for sulfur dissolution and dot formation. The reaction time of 10 h was chosen to enable complete functionalization and ensure a homogeneous particle size distribution. Extended reaction times beyond 12 h did not show significant improvement in fluorescence properties but resulted in minor aggregation. After the hydrothermal reaction, the solution was allowed to cool to room temperature, and centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min was carried out to remove larger or undissolved particles. The supernatant, containing highly fluorescent NH₂-SQDs, was collected and stored at 4 °C in a dark container to prevent photobleaching. The resulting NH₂-SQDs exhibited strong fluorescence stability, a high quantum yield (59.5 %), and enhanced selectivity toward tetracycline due to the optimized synthesis parameters.

The F-7000 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) was employed to measure the fluorescence spectra at an aperture width of 5 nm. The JEM-2100 plus TEM analyzer (JEOL, Japan) was employed to examine the morphology of NH2-SQDs. The Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA) was employed to quantify the functional groups of NH2-SQDs. Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha + spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) was employed to record the XPS spectra of NH2-SQDs. The UV-2100 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to record the UV–Vis absorption spectra. The FLS1000 lifetime spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK) was employed to conduct the decay curves.

To assess the sensitivity of NH2-SQDs toward tetracycline, their interactions with various metal ions and antibiotics were examined in aqueous solutions. The NH2-SQDs were first diluted 100-fold using distilled water. A 200 μL aliquot of the diluted NH2-SQDs solution was then combined with an equal volume of different antibiotics (such as kanamycin, amoxicillin, ampicillin, chlortetracycline, tetracycline, and gentamicin) or metal ions (including Zn²⁺, Mn²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺, Fe³⁺, Co²⁺, and Ca²⁺) in a centrifuge tube and incubated. Fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 335 nm after six minutes. To further investigate the probe's sensitivity, standard solutions of tetracycline at varying concentrations were added to the NH2-SQDs solution. These solutions were analyzed following the same protocol, with all measurements conducted at room temperature. Each experiment was repeated three times to ensure reliability and accuracy.

The food samples were prepared using the techniques listed below. The honey and milk samples were acquired from a nearby Korean supermart to remove the lipids and proteins; the literature used a variety of procedures. First, 2 mL of food samples were blended with 200 μL (10 % v/v) of trichloroacetic acid, and the mixture was sonicated for 20 min. The resulting solution was subsequently centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for ten minutes. Finally, the precipitate was removed by passing a 0.22 μm filter through the supernatant. Tap water was collected directly from the pipe and used in the lab with no unnecessary filtering or treatment. Tetracycline standard samples were prepared and stored using tap water at a variety of concentrations.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) examination was conducted utilizing a cutting-edge X-ray diffractometer that was equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The NH2-SQDs were created by spreading the nanomaterials onto a glass substrate using a drop-casting technique [13–15]. The scanning range was configured from 10° to 80° (2θ) with an increment of 0.02° and a scanning speed of 0.1° per minute. The XRD measurements were performed under normal atmospheric conditions in order to achieve precise phase identification and structural analysis. The resulting diffraction pattern exhibited distinct peaks at 12.24°, 16.85°, 22.29°, 24.53°, and 37.23°, as depicted in Fig. 1 . These observed diffraction angles were in close agreement with the reference data from the Inorganic Crystal Structure Database (ICSD) No. 98–005–2091, corresponding to the standard sulfur (S8) is JCPDS No. 08–0247. The standard card corresponding to the doping elements was incorporated to describe the modified crystal surface characteristics accurately, indicating that the crystalline structure of the SQDs aligns with a known material profile [16]. The corresponding Miller indices (hkl values) associated with these diffraction peaks were determined to be (10−2), (012), (21−2), (300), and (32−2), respectively. These hkl values provide insight into the orientation of the crystal planes that contributed to the diffraction pattern, confirming the highly ordered crystalline nature of the material. The complete analysis reveals that the synthesized NH2-SQDs crystallize in a monoclinic crystal system. This specific crystal system is characterized by three axes of unequal lengths, with one of them being inclined. The chemical composition of the NH2-SQDs was determined to correspond to elemental sulfur (S8), further verifying the structure through the consistency of the XRD results with standard sulfur allotrope data [17]. The sharpness and well-defined nature of the diffraction peaks highlight the high degree of crystallinity in the synthesized NH2-SQDs, affirming their potential for use in applications where well-ordered crystal structures are critical.

FT-IR analysis was conducted further to investigate the surface chemical groups present on NH2-SQDs. As depicted in Fig. 2 , the transmittance peaks in the mid-infrared region provide significant insights into the functional groups attached to the surface. The broad peak observed at 3308.58 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the O–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups. Additionally, the peaks at 2914.67 cm⁻¹, 2852.91 cm⁻¹, and 1453.51 cm⁻¹ are characteristic of C–H stretching vibrations, which suggest the presence of aliphatic hydrocarbon chains [18]. A prominent absorption band at 1233.59 cm⁻¹ is attributed to nitrogen-containing functional groups, specifically N–H vibrations, reflecting the incorporation of nitrogen-based functionalities on the NH2-SQDs' surface [19].

Moreover, the peak observed at 1085.21 cm⁻¹ is linked to either C–O–H or C–N bond vibrations, which suggests the presence of oxygen- or nitrogen-containing groups in close association with carbon atoms. A particular interest is the peak at 626.53 cm⁻¹, which is indicative of sulfur-oxygen bonding, further confirming the functionalization of the NH2-SQDs with sulfur species [20–23]. The detailed examination of the fingerprint region (1000–600 cm⁻¹) reveals significant peaks, such as those near 1250 cm⁻¹, attributed to C–N stretching, and around 800 cm⁻¹, associated with C–S stretching vibrations. These peaks provide clear evidence of sulfur functionalization, likely in the form of sulfur-amino interactions, reinforcing the multifunctional surface chemistry of NH2-SQDs [24]. The combination of FT-IR data with XPS results confirms the presence of various surface functional groups related to sulfur, nitrogen, and oxygen on NH2-SQDs.

The absorbance spectrum shows two distinct peaks. The first peak at 275.31 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition, typically associated with nonbonding to antibonding electronic transitions, as shown in (Fig. 3 a). The second peak at 337.03 nm, labeled as (Sx²⁻), indicates another electronic transition, possibly related to a more conjugated or specific system within the sample, which may correspond to the Sx²⁻ excited state or charge transfer [25]. The consistent observation of the 337.03 nm absorption and excitation energy peak suggests that the luminescence likely arises from the sulfur core lattice structure. This stability in the absorption characteristics implies a direct relationship between the sulfur lattice and the emission properties, indicating that the electronic transitions responsible for the luminescence are closely linked to the inherent structure of the sulfur core [26]. The sulfur lattice may play a crucial role in governing the energy levels and excitonic behavior that produce the observed emission, providing insight into the material's optical properties. The intensity decreases sharply after 350 nm, indicating minimal absorbance in the visible region, suggesting that the sample does not absorb significantly beyond 400 nm. The excitation-independent fluorescence behavior minimizes variations in emission, enhancing reproducibility in complex sample environments. The fluorescence-quenching mechanism, driven by hydrogen bonding and the inner filter effect (IFE), allows selective tetracycline detection with minimal interference [27].

The absorbance and fluorescence spectra together indicate that the sample absorbs strongly in the UV region and emits primarily in the blue-violet region upon UV excitation. The excitation spectrum, represented in green, shows a peak at 343 nm, indicating the optimal excitation wavelength for the fluorescent material [28]. The red curve represents the emission spectrum, showing a peak at 420 nm as depicted in Fig. 3b This suggests that upon excitation at 343 nm, the sample emits light primarily at 420 nm, which is characteristic of blue-violet fluorescence [29]. Fluorescence emission spectra at multiple excitation wavelengths range from 310 nm to 410 nm, as shown in Fig. 3c. A consistent emission peak is observed around 420 nm for all excitation wavelengths, although the intensity varies with each wavelength. The highest intensity is observed with excitation at 340 nm, while other excitation wavelengths (310 nm to 410 nm) show a general decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelength moves further away from the optimal excitation range. This pattern suggests that the fluorescence emission is relatively stable in terms of peak position but sensitive to excitation wavelength, with maximal efficiency in the near-UV range [30–32]. The stability of the emission wavelength across different excitation wavelengths suggests that the emitting state is relatively unaffected by excitation energy, indicating a well-defined fluorophore with minimal energy relaxation before emission.

The complete surface passivation and excitation-independent behavior of NH2-SQDs could be key factors contributing to their remarkably high quantum yield. Surface passivation helps minimize surface defects, which are often sites for non-radiative recombination, thereby enhancing the efficiency of photon emission. Additionally, the excitation-independent behavior indicates that the quantum dots maintain consistent photoluminescence efficiency regardless of the excitation energy [33]. This stability across different excitation wavelengths ensures that the quantum yield remains high under various conditions, making NH2-SQDs highly efficient for applications in optoelectronics and bioimaging. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) measurements indicated a high quantum yield of 59.5 %, which can be attributed to the fast reaction time, high PLQY, and straightforward purification process. This method presents a promising approach for the large-scale production of ultrabright fluorescent NH2-SQDs using more affordable sulfur-based materials. Sheng et al. [34] achieved a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 58.6 % for SQDs synthesized through a one-pot method using ultrasonic microwave irradiation, attributing the high PLQY to the size-focusing mechanism. The results indicate that the synthesized NH2-SQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence properties and excellent hydrophilicity, making them particularly suitable for various applications. The highly photoluminescent nature of these NH2-SQDs is especially valuable for fields such as sensing and biomedical applications [35]. As a result, these NH2-SQDs have great potential to be developed into efficient tools for real-time imaging and diagnostics in biomedical research and clinical applications.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) image showing NH2-SQDs distributed across the field at a relatively low magnification (scale bar: 20 nm), as shown in Fig 4 a. The image likely highlights the uniform distribution of NH2-SQDs on a substrate or within a medium. The NH2-SQDs are well dispersed, showing no significant aggregation. The clarity and even spread suggest good control over synthesis parameters. A high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image zoomed in on an individual nanoparticle (scale bar: 5 nm). The measurement of 0.23 nm between lattice fringes is indicative of the crystallographic spacing (lattice planes) of the nanoparticle material, which can be used to identify the crystalline structure, as shown in Fig. 4b The clear atomic-scale resolution of the lattice fringes confirms the crystalline nature of the NH2-SQDs, which is essential for understanding properties like electronic structure and core of the sulfur [36]. The particle size distribution in Fig. 4c confirms that the NH2-SQDs are relatively small, in the range of 1.5 to 4 nm, with most particles centered around 2.3 nm. This size distribution, along with the crystalline properties observed, implies that the synthesis method used yields NH2-SQDs with controlled sizes and shapes, which is important for reproducibility in their applications [37].

The chemical composition and bonding states of NH2-SQDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum (Fig. 5 a) shows distinct peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p, indicating the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur in the NH2-SQDs. These elements' relative atomic percentages are shown in Fig. 5b, with carbon (68.43 %), nitrogen (17.19 %), oxygen (8.12 %), and sulfur (6.26 %). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) was deconvoluted into three components. The major peak at 284.60 eV is attributed to sp²-hybridized carbon (CC), indicating the graphitic nature of the material. A second peak at ∼285.88 eV corresponds to CN bonding, suggesting nitrogen incorporation into the carbon matrix. The third peak at ∼288.20 eV is assigned to carbon-oxygen species, such as C = O, due to the oxidation on the surface [38]. The deconvoluted N1s spectrum (Fig. 5d) presents two peaks. The primary peak at 399.48 eV is assigned to pyridinic nitrogen (N-pyr), while the smaller peak at 401.71 eV is associated with (S-N), confirming nitrogen doping into the sulfur core. These nitrogen species are known to enhance the material's electronic properties, potentially contributing to catalytic activity [39]. To further investigate the potential sulfur states present on the surface of the NH2-SQDs, a detailed analysis of the deconvoluted S2p spectrum (Fig. 5e) was conducted. The S2p binding energy peaks were observed at 161.66 eV and 162.94 eV, which are characteristic of elemental sulfur (S²⁻) and indicate the presence of sulfur in a reduced state, commonly associated with sulfur in thiophene-like or sulfide environments. These peaks suggest that sulfur atoms are covalently bonded within the quantum dot structure, contributing to the overall stability of the material.

Additionally, higher binding energy peaks were identified at 167.58 eV and 168.72 eV. These peaks are attributed to oxidized sulfur species, specifically SO₃²⁻ (sulfonate) and SO₂²⁻ (sulfite), respectively. The presence of these oxidized sulfur species indicates that partial oxidation of sulfur has occurred on the surface of the NH2-SQDs. Such oxidation may result from exposure to air or other environmental factors during sample processing. The coexistence of both reduced (S²⁻) and oxidized sulfur species on the surface implies complex sulfur chemistry, which could influence the surface reactivity and electronic properties of the quantum dots [40]. This distribution of sulfur states, including both elemental and oxidized forms, plays a critical role in modifying the surface characteristics of the NH2-SQDs. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 5f) reveals two distinct peaks. The peak at 531.03 eV corresponds to oxygen in carbonyl groups (C = O), while the peak at 532.80 eV is attributed to oxygen in hydroxyl groups (-OH) or adsorbed water, indicating that surface oxidation has occurred, likely during NH2-SQDs preparation [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: NH₂-SQD as a fluorescent probe for tetracycline detection
ce:section.ce:para: An amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH₂-SQDs) selective fluorescent probe for the determination of tetracycline antibiotics. The NH₂-SQD samples in solution media were exposed to a range of Kanamycin (Kan), Amoxicillin (Amox), Ampicillin (Amp), Chlortetracycline (CTC), Tetracycline and Gentamicin (Gen) and other common ions (Zn²⁺, Mn²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺, Fe³⁺, Co²⁺, and Ca²⁺ The fluorescence intensity response of NH₂-SQDs to each antibiotic or ion was examined using an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. As can be seen in (Fig. 6 a), only tetracycline led to the fluorescence quenching of NH₂-SQDs significantly, indicating high selectivity and strong anti-interference ability of the method for TC detection. This selectivity implies that NH₂-SQDs hold great potential as fluorescent probes for the determination of tetracycline residues in water samples [42]. As shown in (Fig. 6b,c), the fluorescence intensity of NH₂-SQDs decreases from 0.1 to 100 μmol/L of tetracycline concentration, with a correlation of linearity between the fluorescence quenching ratio F/F0 and tetracycline. Here, F is the fluorescence intensity after the addition of tetracycline, and F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of the tetracycline. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated using the formula 3*σ/S (3 × 0.00253/2.5217). Where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank responses or baseline noise, reflecting the level of random fluctuations in the signal, and S is the slope of the calibration curve. The observed linear regression line fit was obtained in a calibration range of 0.1 to 60 μmol/L for tetracycline with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9998 and a lower detection limit of 0.003 μmol/L from (Fig 6d). Therefore, this method was effective in sensing tetracycline from water samples. Photographic images illustrating the selective confirmation of tetracycline, as shown in Fig. 6e.

Tetracycline has been detected using NH₂-SQDs via a fluorescence-quenching mechanism that provides high sensing sensitivity and selectivity. When tetracycline interacts with NH₂-SQDs, it induces fluorescence quenching through two primary pathways. The identification of static quenching through complexation and inner filter effect (IFE). In the static quenching pathway, the surface amine groups of NH₂-SQDs form hydrogen bonding interactions with the functional groups of tetracycline, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups. These interactions are hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces that result in the formation of a ground state of a complex between NH₂-SQDs and tetracycline. This complex changes the electronic environment of the NH₂-SQDs, and as a consequence, their inherent fluorescence is quenched, thereby reducing the emission intensity. This formation of the ground-state complex implies that fluorescence quenching does not require molecular collisions in the excited state and is, hence, efficient when targeting low tetracycline concentrations. The inner filter effect (IFE) increases the sensitivity of the NH₂-SQDs sensor to the analyzed tetracycline [43].

The fluorescence intensity, a part of the emitted light, is reabsorbed by tetracycline, and thus, the intensity of observed emission appears to be lower. The extent of quenching as a result of IFE is proportional to the concentration of tetracycline, suggesting another efficiency of NH₂-SQDs in the determination of tetracycline content in a solution. These two mechanisms collectively allowed the NH₂-SQDs to selectivity and high sensitivity as a fluorescence-based sensor for tetracycline detection. The allostery of the dual quenching effect affords a linear relationship between the intensity of quenching fluorescence and the concentration of tetracycline for quantitative analysis. Thirdly, selective adsorption of tetracycline over other structurally related compounds is also made possible by the ability of NH₂-SQDs to interact with the functional groups of tetracycline, limited by the interference from other coexisting substances present in environmental and biological samples. Because of those characteristics, NH₂-SQDs have a great potential to be used for tetracycline detection in environmental water samples that contain antibiotics and in biomedical practices, where it is vital to measure antibiotic levels accurately. The exclusion of other substances forming hydrogen bonds with NH₂-SQDs is justified by the combination of selective fluorescence quenching, the dual quenching mechanism (hydrogen bonding and IFE), structural compatibility, experimental controls, and real-sample validation. These factors collectively ensure the specificity of NH₂-SQDs for TC detection. Given the sensitivity and selectivity of NH₂-SQDs, these quantum dots provide a powerful instrument for monitoring and analyzing tetracycline in real-time. Thus, they contribute to the comprehension of the processes occurring in different environments and enhance opportunities for diagnostics of human states of health [44].

Fluorescence lifetime decay analysis of amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH₂-SQDs) in the presence of tetracycline (TC) indicates that the quenching mechanism is mainly static, as shown in Fig. 7 . The decay profiles change little after the addition of TC, implying that NH₂-SQDs and TC form a stable ground-state complex without changing the excited-state lifetime, which is typical behavior for static quenching since it applied the decrease in fluorescence intensity due to the formation of complexes before excitation. This slight reduction in amplitude supports that conclusion even more. Differential quenching, which is based on collisional contact and consistently inhibits the fluorescence lifetime, is excluded as the prevailing process to explain the lifetime not changing. These results are consistent with previous literature, which shows that NH₂-SQDs form significant interactions with TC through strong electrostatic and hydrogen-bonding attractions, prompting effective static quenching. To this end, the data that the one method cannot explain at once can be validated by Stern-Volmer analysis and UV–Vis absorption spectra; this process can help to take full advantage of NH₂-SQDs with excellent selectivity and sensitivity and eventually can further promote the applications of NH₂-SQDs as reliable fluorescent probes to detect tetracycline in food samples.

In order to improve the sensing performance of amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH₂-SQDs) for tetracycline detection, an investigation of sensing factors was conducted. This present work aimed at comprehensively studying incubation time, pH, NaCl concentration, choice of solvent, and incubation time to enhance the performance of NH₂-SQDs as a fluorescent probe was analyzed. Quencher values increasing over incubation time 0 to 50 min, defined high initial quenching in the first 20 min and equilibration at 25 min was obtained by spiking of tetracycline. This time taken for effective interaction of tetracycline with NH₂-SQDs was identified by effective quenching efficiency. The incubation time of 25 min was proposed to be a feasible time frame to achieve maximum efficiency in detecting tetracycline, as illustrated in (Fig. 8 a). The fluorescence quenching behavior of NH₂-SQDs was tested in the pH 2.0 to 12.0. The investigators noted that maximum quenching was with buffer solutions located at a neutral to slightly alkaline range of pH 7.0 to 8.0, beyond which there was a decline in quenching efficiency [45], which may be attributed to the effect of protonation or deprotonation of functional groups of NH₂-SQDs and tetracycline, affecting the electrostatic properties and bonding. A pH of 7.0 was thus used in other experiments to allow efficient quenching while preserving the structure of both the NH₂-SQDs and tetracycline molecules for accurate detection in practical applications, as depicted in (Fig. 8b).

To the ionic strength typically observed in environmental and biological samples, quenching was performed with different NaCl concentrations, ranging from 0 to 1.0 M. The quenching efficiency only slightly decreases with increasing NaCl concentration above 0.4 M. This decline must probably be due to ionic screening that hinders the electrostatic interactions between NH₂-SQDs and tetracycline. Thus, a 0.4 M NaCl concentration was found to be optimal for the detection of tetracycline under moderate ionic strength conditions for better quenching ability, as shown in (Fig. 8c). To assess further the effect of various solvents on the NH₂-SQDs and tetracycline interaction, fluorescence quenching measurements were carried in deionized water, ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile and acetone [46]. The NH₂-SQDs had the highest quenching efficiency in deionized water and ethanol and the least efficiency in methanol and acetonitrile, as depicted in (Fig. 8d). The difference is hence attributed to β-β stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions that are disrupted in the latter solvents and may hamper quenching. Therefore, deionized water was considered the optimal solvent to meet the high sensitivity and effectiveness of the subsequent analysis under laboratory and real-sample tests [47]. Compared to earlier tetracycline sensors based on quantum dots, as shown in Table 1 .

Amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NH₂-SQDs) were evaluated for their effectiveness in detecting tetracycline (TC) in food samples, including milk, honey, and water. The process began with sample collection and the removal of particulate matter to ensure clarity in fluorescence measurements. A preliminary test was conducted to confirm the absence of naturally occurring tetracycline in the samples before controlled spiking experiments. To simulate real-world contamination, known concentrations of tetracycline were introduced into the samples. Fluorescence-based detection was carried out at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, where tetracycline-induced fluorescence quenching was monitored. The concentration of tetracycline in the spiked samples was determined using a pre-established linear relationship between tetracycline levels and fluorescence intensity reduction. The results demonstrated high accuracy and reproducibility, with recovery rates ranging from 98.2 % to 99.7 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) between 1.32 % and 1.46 %. These values confirm the method's precision across different matrices, as shown in Table 2 .

Amine-functionalized sulfur quantum dots (NH₂-SQDs) as an efficient and selective fluorescent probe for tetracycline detection in food and environmental samples. The NH₂-SQDs demonstrated superior photoluminescence stability, high quantum yield (59.5 %), and strong selectivity, making them a promising tool for real-time monitoring of antibiotic residues. The dual fluorescence quenching mechanism, involving hydrogen bonding and the inner filter effect (IFE), enables highly sensitive detection with a low detection limit of 0.003 μmol/L. Furthermore, real-sample validation in milk, honey, and water confirmed the probe's effectiveness, with high recovery rates (94.7–100.5 %) and minimal interference. Despite these promising results, several limitations need to be addressed further to improve the practical applicability of NH₂-SQDs in tetracycline detection. The detection limit achieved in this study is highly competitive; additional modifications to the surface chemistry of NH₂-SQDs could enhance their fluorescence response and improve sensitivity even further. Future research should explore alternative functionalization strategies, such as incorporating additional heteroatoms or optimizing ligand structures, to achieve even lower detection limits. The NH₂-SQDs demonstrated strong selectivity for tetracycline over other antibiotics and metal ions; potential interference from structurally similar compounds in complex food matrices warrants further investigation. Additional testing in a broader range of real-world samples, including meat and egg products, would provide deeper insights into the robustness and specificity of this detection method. Another key challenge is the speed of detection. Although NH₂-SQDs exhibit a relatively rapid response time, further optimization is required to develop a truly real-time, field-deployable sensor. The integration of NH₂-SQDs with microfluidic or portable fluorescence-based devices could enable faster and more convenient on-site detection. Exploring alternative readout techniques, such as smartphone-based fluorescence imaging, may offer practical advantages for field applications. The long-term stability of NH₂-SQDs under different environmental conditions requires further investigation to ensure consistent performance over time. Factors such as photobleaching, pH variations, and temperature fluctuations could affect the fluorescence properties of NH₂-SQDs. Developing protective coatings or embedding NH₂-SQDs into stable sensing platforms could enhance durability and extend their usability in real-world applications.
Jothi Vinoth Kumar: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).
